title: Invite code is pure ULID timestamp — zero random bits, predictable
description: In space_invite_set: 'let code = Ulid::new().to_string()[..8].to_uppercase()'. A ULID's first 10 chars encode the 48-bit millisecond timestamp; [..8] slices only the timestamp portion — zero random bits. Two invites created in the same millisecond are identical. An attacker who knows approximately when an invite was created (~day granularity) can enumerate ~86M codes to find it (2^26 search space). Fix: use the random suffix: Ulid::new().to_string()[10..18] (8 chars of 80-bit random) or rand::thread_rng() directly.
close_reason: Fixed: changed [..8] to [10..18] to use ULID random suffix (80-bit) instead of timestamp prefix (zero random bits)
